Title: Google Antigravity Documentation
URL: https://antigravity.google/docs/cli/plugins
Published: N/A
Author: N/A
Highlights:
Extend agent capabilities, install third-party extension bundles, package custom workflow skills, and interface with Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers.
...
Antigravity CLI is designed for limitless customization. You can augment the shared agent harness by installing structured package modules called Plugins or creating localized markdown blueprints called Skills.
...
Plugins are namespaced bundles that package custom skills, background subagents, linting rules, Model Context Protocol definitions, and event hooks into a single deployable asset.
...
```
~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/
...
_name>/
...
├── plugin.json                 # Required package marker file
├── mcp_config.json             # Optional Model Context Protocol servers
├── hooks.json                  # Optional pre/post tool event hooks
├── skills/                     # Optional specialized skills directory
├── agents/                     # Optional subagent definition templates
└── rules/                      # Optional custom codebase rules files
...
The`plugin.json` file is a mandatory manifest located at the root of your plugin directory. It defines the plugin's identity and metadata.
...
The CLI exposes a`plugin`(or plural`plugins`) subcommand pipeline to manage your extensions:
...
Skills are declarative, human-readable markdown files that outline explicit instruction protocols, scripts, and target resources for specialized engineering tasks.
...
Once registered, Skills convert automatically into slash commands inside the TUI, allowing you to invoke them manually (e.g., typing`/refactor-ui`).
...
1. Create a directory named`.agents/skills/` at your project root.
2. Inside, draft a markdown file with a`.md` extension (such as`format-tests.md`).
3. Define the skill's Frontmatter metadata (see the example below).
4. Below the metadata, write explicit instructions for the agent. When you run`agy` in this directory, the skill is compiled, and`/format-tests` becomes available in the prompt box.
...
Any markdown skill in this directory is automatically imported as a global slash command whenever you launch`agy` in any directory.
...
Hooks intercept agent actions right before or immediately after execution. They are useful for running automated pre-flight checks or post
...
generation formats (such as
...
Model Context Protocol is an open standard enabling foundation models to interface securely with local APIs, file parsers, and custom developer tools.
...
For comprehensive documentation on configuring local and remote MCP servers in Antigravity CLI, accessing the interactive`/mcp` manager overlay, and understanding server schemas and authentication, see the dedicated MCP Documentation.

Title: From Extensions to Plugins: Migrating Your Gemini CLI Extensions to Antigravity Plugins | by Denise Kwan | Jun, 2026 | Medium
URL: https://medium.com/@denisetkwan/from-extensions-to-plugins-migrating-your-gemini-cli-extensions-to-antigravity-plugins-49df7aa9c960
Published: 2026-06-16T20:05:23.000Z
Author: Denise Kwan
Highlights:
The mandatory migration from Gemini CLI to Antigravity CLI is approaching on June 18th for individual plans (Free, Google AI Pro, Google AI Ultra). To ensure a smooth transition, we recommend reviewing the official migration guide and the Migrating to Antigravity CLI blog post. With over 1,000 extensions in the Gemini CLI ecosystem, many developers rely on these tools to maintain a seamless, integrated workflow. This guide will walk you through migrating your extensions to Antigravity plugins — whether you are trying to keep your existing extensions, installing new ones, or transitioning an extension you built.
...
While the change involves a new naming convention, it is important to note that Gemini CLI extensions and Antigravity plugins are fundamentally the same in purpose and core functionality. Both serve as the primary mechanism for extending the capabilities of the CLI agent, allowing developers to integrate custom tools. The shift primarily introduces a different structural and organizational framework to improve how these capabilities are defined and managed within the ecosystem. Let’s take a look at the differences.
...
- `gemini-extension.json` is replaced with`plugin.json` and`mcp_config.json`.
- `agents/` and`commands/` folders are removed.
- `hooks.json` is moved directly under the root folder,`plugin-name`, instead of a`hooks/` subdirectory.
- Context files, including`GEMINI.md`, are moved under the`rules/` subdirectory.
...
At this time, this feature is only compatible with Gemini CLI extension repositories that do not contain local MCP servers.
...
2. Copy the`convert-extension-to-plugin` directory into your Antigravity skills folder:
...
This skill automates the migration of your extension codebase to the Antigravity plugin ecosystem. It handles configuration mapping, command-to-skill translation, hook relocations, release script updates, and dependencies packaging.
...
5. If you have a local MCP as part of your extension, add a note to your`README.md` for users to replace the [YOUR_PLUGIN_PATH] in the`mcp_config.json` file to the path of their plugin. At this time, Antigravity plugins do not support variables or relative urls.
...
- The contents of`gemini-extension.json` are split between`plugin.json` and`mcp_config.json`.
- Replace any usage of Gemini CLI specific variables such as`${extensionPath}`,`${workspacePath}`, and`${/}` with the absolute path.
- Custom commands in Gemini CLI extensions need to be converted into individual skills.
...
- The contents in the`hooks/` subdirectory need to be moved directly to the root directory of the plugin and the`hooks/` directory needs to be deleted.
...
- Context files, including`GEMINI.md` need to be moved under the`rules/` subdirectory.

Title: Configuring MCP Servers and Skills for Antigravity CLI and IDE | by Dazbo (Darren Lester) | Google Cloud - Community | May, 2026 | Medium
URL: https://medium.com/google-cloud/configuring-mcp-servers-and-skills-for-antigravity-cli-and-ide-a938c7eebb78
Published: 2026-05-24T10:36:37.000Z
Author: Dazbo (Darren Lester)
Highlights:
4. Antigravity CLI, which — somewhat controversially — replaces the hugely popular and open-source Gemini CLI. It’s still a terminal-first environment for interacting with Gemini models. But the new Antigravity CLI is built in Go, and you can tell; it feels much faster than Gemini CLI, both during startup and in general use. It leverages the same agent “harness” as Antigravity 2.0 and the IDE, and this allows for common settings and configuration across the Antigravity suite.
...
I can understand Google’s rationale for bringing Gemini CLI into the Antigravity suite. Previously these products were developed independently and in parallel. As a result they had different standards and approaches for configuration (such as MCP servers), and where to put things… like skills. This creates a certain amount of friction for users. Now, in theory, we only need to configure in one place.
...
- At the time of writing, the official Antigravity documentation says that to configure skills “globally”, you need to put them here:`~/.gemini/antigravity/skills/ /`
...
- But my experience is that this is incorrect. Antigravity tools do not pick up skills placed in this location. Instead, you should put your`skills` folder at the top of your`.gemini` folder. We can confirm this by running the`/skills` command from inside Agy CLI:
...
- You can see that “global” skills — meaning those available to all workspaces when running Agy CLI only — should go in`~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills`, whereas “shared” skills — meaning those available to all workspaces running any Antigravity tools — should go in`~/.gemini/skills`.
...
- If we want to share MCP server configurations across Antigravity tools, you need to create`mcp_config.json` file and place it in the new`~/.gemini/config` folder. (This is where “shared” extensions — now called “plugins” go also.)
...
- You need to use “serverUrl”, not the older “httpUrl”
- The “timeout” parameter is no longer supported at the top level of each MCP server.
- Inline comments are not supported.
...
These commands install skills to your`~/.agents/skills` folder. But I want them to be in`~/.gemini/skills`. As mentioned previously, this location is picked up by all Agy products. So, go ahead and move the skills you’ve installed. Or you can run a script like this to do it for you:
...
Migrating from the old Gemini CLI to the new Antigravity ecosystem does require a bit of mental rewiring when it comes to folder structures. But having a single, unified source of truth for your MCP servers and skills is a leap forward. No more duplicating configurations or wondering why a custom skill works in your terminal but fails in your IDE. (Hopefully!)

Title: joegoldin/antigravity-cli-nix
URL: https://github.com/joegoldin/antigravity-cli-nix
Published: 2026-05-21T00:17:46.000Z
Author: N/A
Highlights:
Declarative Antigravity CLI plugins, skills, agents, rules, hooks, and MCP servers for Nix
...
Declarative [Antigravity CLI](https://antigravity.google/docs/cli-overview)
plugins, skills, agents, rules, hooks, and MCP servers for Nix.

Successor to [gemini-nix](https://github.com/joegoldin/gemini-nix), which is
archived (it targeted the deprecated `gemini-cli`).
...
| Function | Returns | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `mkSkill { name; description; allowed-tools? []; extraFiles? []; } body` | derivation | `$out/skills/ /SKILL.md` |
| `mkAgent { name; description; } body` | derivation | `$out/agents/.md` |
| `mkRule { name; } body` | derivation | `$out/rules/.md` |
| `mkHook { event; matcher? ""; name; command; timeout?; description?; }` | attrset | Collected into per-plugin `hooks.json` |
| `mkMcpServer { name; ...; }` | attrset | Collected into per-plugin `mcp_config.json` |
| `mkPlugin { name; description?; skills?; agents?; rules?; hooks?; mcpServers?; }` | derivation | `pkgs.buildEnv` with `plugin.json` + per-plugin `hooks.json` + `mcp_config.json` |
| `mkAgy { package; extraArgs? []; env? {}; }` | `writeShellScriptBin` | Optional `agy` wrapper |
...
```nix
programs.antigravity-cli-nix = {
  enable        = true;                              # install antigravity + manage config
  package       = pkgs.llm-agents.antigravity;       # default
  plugins       = [ ... ];                           # list of mkPlugin derivations
  settings      = { ... };                           # ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/settings.json
  mcpServers    = { ... };                           # global mcp_config.json (see below)
  extraPackages = [ ... ];                           # extra packages installed alongside
};
```
...
Each plugin's `passthru.meta.name` determines its install path under
`~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/plugins/ /`. Settings are merged into the
existing `settings.json` via `jq -s '.[0] * .[1]'` (user-managed keys
preserved).
...
### MCP servers
...
`programs.antigravity-cli-nix.mcpServers` writes `{ "mcpServers": { ... } }` to
each path in `mcpConfigPaths` (defaults to both
`~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/mcp_config.json` and `~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json`,
since Antigravity's global MCP path is mid-migration and both load).
Remote servers use `serverUrl` (not `url`/`httpUrl`).
...
```nix
programs.antigravity-cli-nix.mcpServers = {
  context7 = { command = "npx"; args = [ "-y" "@upstash/context7-mcp" ]; };
  dev-knowledge = { serverUrl = "https://developerknowledge.googleapis.com/mcp"; };
};
...
Merged via the same `jq` deep-merge as `settings.json`; skipped entirely when
empty. MCP lives in a dedicated `mcp_config.json` — Antigravity does not read
server definitions from `settings.json`.

`GEMINI.md` is Antigravity-specific and lives in your project root. It applies only to that specific project.`AGENTS.md` is a cross-tool standard that works across Antigravity, Claude Code, and other agentic IDEs that have adopted the format. For project-specific rules, use`GEMINI.md`. For rules you want to apply globally across all your projects and tools, use`AGENTS.md` in your home directory.
